questions.question:
Discuss the call of Abraham and its significance.
(a) What was God's command to Abraham in Genesis 12:1-3?
(b) State THREE promises God made to Abraham.
(c) State THREE lessons Christians can learn from Abraham's call.
questions.answer:
(a) God's command to Abraham (Genesis 12:1-3):
God commanded Abraham (then called Abram) to:
— Leave his country (Ur of the Chaldeans)
— Leave his people and his father's household
— Go to a land that God would show him

This was a call to complete faith and obedience — Abraham did not know where he was going; he simply trusted God.

(b) Three promises God made to Abraham:
1. God promised to make him a great nation.
2. God promised to bless him and make his name great.
3. God promised that through him all peoples on earth would be blessed (the messianic promise fulfilled in Jesus Christ).
4. God promised to bless those who bless him and curse those who curse him.

(c) Three lessons Christians can learn:
1. Faith requires obedience — Abraham obeyed without knowing the final destination, teaching Christians to trust God's direction even when uncertain.
2. God's promises are reliable — Abraham received and held onto God's promises for years; Christians are encouraged to trust God's word even when fulfilment is delayed.
3. Leaving comfort zones for God — Abraham left his homeland and security; Christians may be called to make sacrifices for God's purposes.
4. God blesses those who obey Him — Abraham's obedience led to immense blessing, encouraging Christians to prioritise God's call over personal comfort.

questions.question:
Narrate the story of the Exodus and explain its religious significance.
(a) How did Moses lead Israel out of Egypt?
(b) State THREE plagues God sent on Egypt.
(c) Explain the significance of the Passover.
questions.answer:
(a) Moses leading Israel out of Egypt:
God appeared to Moses in a burning bush and commissioned him to lead Israel out of Egyptian slavery. Moses, with his brother Aaron, confronted Pharaoh repeatedly, demanding Israel's release. God sent ten plagues on Egypt because Pharaoh repeatedly hardened his heart and refused to let the Israelites go.

After the tenth plague (death of the firstborn), Pharaoh finally released the Israelites. Moses led approximately 600,000 men plus women and children out of Egypt. When Pharaoh changed his mind and pursued them with his army, God miraculously parted the Red Sea, allowing Israel to cross on dry ground. When the Egyptian army followed, the waters closed over them, destroying Pharaoh's army.

(b) Three of the ten plagues:
1. Water turned to blood — All water in Egypt turned to blood, making it undrinkable.
2. Plague of locusts — Swarms of locusts devoured all crops and vegetation.
3. Darkness — Three days of complete darkness covered Egypt.
4. Death of the firstborn — The final and most devastating plague: every firstborn in Egypt died.
5. (Others: frogs, gnats, flies, livestock disease, boils, hail and fire)

(c) Significance of the Passover:
On the night of the tenth plague, God instructed each Israelite family to slaughter a lamb, apply its blood to the doorposts, and eat the meal with their sandals on, ready to leave. When God passed through Egypt, He would 'pass over' any house marked with blood, sparing the firstborn.

Religious significance:
1. The Passover symbolises God's redemption and deliverance — He saved His people from slavery.
2. It foreshadows the sacrifice of Jesus Christ — John the Baptist called Jesus 'the Lamb of God who takes away the sin of the world'.
3. It established the covenant relationship between God and Israel.
4. It is still celebrated by Jews today as a reminder of God's faithfulness.

questions.question:
Discuss the ministry of the prophet Amos.
(a) Who was Amos and what was his background?
(b) State THREE social evils Amos condemned.
(c) What is Amos's message for Christians today?
questions.answer:
(a) Who was Amos:
Amos was a prophet from Tekoa, a small town south of Jerusalem in Judah. He was NOT a professional prophet or the son of a prophet. He was a shepherd and a dresser of sycamore fig trees — a common man called by God to deliver a message to the Northern Kingdom of Israel during the reign of King Jeroboam II (around 760 BCE). He ministered at a time of great prosperity in Israel but also great moral corruption.

(b) Three social evils Amos condemned:
1. Oppression of the poor — The wealthy were trampling on the poor, selling the needy for a pair of sandals, exploiting the helpless (Amos 2:6-7). The rich used unjust business practices to accumulate wealth at the expense of the poor.

2. Corruption in the courts — Justice was perverted; judges accepted bribes and denied the poor a fair hearing. The righteous were punished while the wicked went free.

3. Religious hypocrisy — Israel observed religious festivals and offered sacrifices while continuing to live wickedly. God rejected their worship, saying He hated their feasts and wanted justice to flow like a river instead.

4. Luxury and self-indulgence — The wealthy lived in lavish houses, ate the best food, and were at ease in Zion while the poor suffered.

(c) Amos's message for Christians today:
1. Social justice is central to true religion — God demands that we care for the poor and vulnerable, not just observe religious rituals.
2. Worship without righteous living is unacceptable — Christians must match their worship with ethical behaviour in daily life.
3. God holds the powerful accountable — Leaders and the wealthy will answer to God for how they treat those under their authority.
4. Christians must speak out against injustice — Like Amos, ordinary Christians are called to challenge social wrongs even at personal cost.

questions.question:
Discuss the Sermon on the Mount (Matthew 5-7).
(a) Name and explain FIVE of the Beatitudes.
(b) Explain what Jesus meant by 'You are the salt of the earth and the light of the world'.
(c) State TWO ways the Sermon on the Mount is relevant to Christians today.
questions.answer:
(a) Five Beatitudes (Matthew 5:3-10):

1. Blessed are the poor in spirit, for theirs is the kingdom of heaven.
Meaning: Those who recognise their spiritual emptiness and total dependence on God are truly blessed.

2. Blessed are those who mourn, for they will be comforted.
Meaning: Those who grieve over sin and suffering will receive God's comfort and consolation.

3. Blessed are the meek, for they will inherit the earth.
Meaning: Those who are humble, gentle, and self-controlled — not self-assertive — will receive God's promised inheritance.

4. Blessed are those who hunger and thirst for righteousness, for they will be filled.
Meaning: Those who deeply desire to live rightly before God will be satisfied by Him.

5. Blessed are the merciful, for they will be shown mercy.
Meaning: Those who show compassion and forgiveness to others will receive God's mercy.

6. Blessed are the pure in heart, for they will see God.
Meaning: Those with sincere, undivided hearts toward God will experience His presence.

7. Blessed are the peacemakers, for they will be called children of God.
Meaning: Those who actively pursue reconciliation and peace reflect God's character.

(b) Salt of the earth and light of the world:
Salt of the earth: Just as salt preserves food from decay and adds flavour, Christians are called to preserve moral goodness in society and add meaning to people's lives through their character and influence.

Light of the world: Just as light dispels darkness, Christians are called to live visibly good lives that illuminate the way of God for others. Jesus said their good deeds should be visible so that people glorify God.

(c) Relevance today:
1. The Beatitudes provide a counter-cultural value system — in a world that values power and wealth, Jesus blesses the humble, merciful, and pure-hearted.
2. The teaching on love for enemies (Matthew 5:43-48) is relevant to conflict resolution, reconciliation, and peacebuilding in Nigeria and globally.

questions.question:
Discuss the conversion of Paul and its impact on the early church.
(a) Narrate the account of Paul's conversion on the road to Damascus.
(b) How did Paul's conversion change his life and ministry?
(c) State THREE contributions of Paul to the early church.
questions.answer:
(a) Paul's conversion (Acts 9:1-19):
Saul of Tarsus was a devout Pharisee and a fierce persecutor of Christians. He had witnessed and approved the stoning of Stephen and was travelling to Damascus with letters from the high priest authorising him to arrest Christians and bring them to Jerusalem.

On the way to Damascus, a blinding light from heaven suddenly flashed around him and he fell to the ground. He heard a voice saying: 'Saul, Saul, why do you persecute me?' Saul asked who was speaking and the voice replied: 'I am Jesus, whom you are persecuting.'

Saul was blinded for three days and neither ate nor drank. In Damascus, God sent a disciple named Ananias to him. Despite initial reluctance (knowing Saul's reputation), Ananias obeyed God, placed his hands on Saul, and his sight was restored. Saul was then baptised and immediately began preaching that Jesus is the Son of God.

(b) How conversion changed Paul's life:
1. He changed from a persecutor to a passionate preacher of Christianity.
2. His entire worldview transformed — he went from trusting in the law and his own righteousness to trusting in the grace of Jesus Christ.
3. He embraced suffering for the gospel, experiencing imprisonment, beatings, shipwreck, and eventually martyrdom.

(c) Three contributions to the early church:
1. Missionary journeys — Paul undertook three major missionary journeys, establishing churches across Asia Minor, Greece, and Rome, spreading Christianity throughout the Roman Empire.
2. New Testament letters — Paul wrote 13 epistles (letters) that form a major part of the New Testament, providing theological teaching, pastoral guidance, and ethical instruction.
3. Theology of grace — Paul articulated the doctrine of justification by faith (not works of the law), which became central to Christian theology and later the Protestant Reformation.
